title: Java Cheatsheet
description: Master Java with clear, memorable concepts — explained like a friend teaching you

concepts.name: Your First Java Program
concepts.explanation: Think of this as Java's 'welcome ceremony.' Every program needs a class (your code's home) and a main method (the front door where Java always starts). The class is the building, main() is where you enter.
keyPoints: public = anyone can access this (like a public park)
static = belongs to the class itself, not any specific object
void = this method gives nothing back when it finishes
main = the magic name Java looks for to start running
String[] args = optional inputs you can pass when running the program

concepts.name: Primitive Data Types
concepts.explanation: These are Java's building blocks — simple values stored directly in memory. Think of them as different-sized boxes: byte is a tiny box, int is medium, long is huge. Use the smallest box that fits to save memory.
concepts.code:
// Whole numbers (pick based on size needed)
byte tiny = 127;           // -128 to 127 (1 byte)
short small = 32000;       // -32,768 to 32,767 (2 bytes)
int normal = 2_000_000;    // Most common choice (4 bytes)
long huge = 9_000_000_000L;// Add 'L' suffix! (8 bytes)

// Decimal numbers
float price = 19.99f;      // Add 'f' suffix! (4 bytes)
double precise = 3.14159;  // Default for decimals (8 bytes)

// Others
boolean isReady = true;    // Only true or false
char letter = 'A';         // Single character in single quotes
keyPoints: int is your go-to for whole numbers — 99% of the time, it's enough
double is default for decimals — only use float when memory is critical
Don't forget L for long and f for float — the compiler needs a hint!
Use underscores in big numbers for readability: 1_000_000 = 1000000

concepts.name: Reading User Input
concepts.explanation: Scanner is like a microphone for your program — it listens to what the user types. Import it, point it at System.in (the keyboard), and use different methods based on what you expect. Always close it when done!
concepts.code:
import java.util.Scanner;  // Step 1: Import

Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);  // Step 2: Create

System.out.print("What's your name? ");  // print (no newline)
String name = scanner.nextLine();  // Reads entire line

System.out.print("How old are you? ");
int age = scanner.nextInt();       // Reads just the number

scanner.close();  // Step 3: Close when done!
keyPoints: nextLine() = reads everything until Enter (best for names, sentences)
nextInt()/nextDouble() = reads just the number, leaves the Enter behind
Common bug fix: after nextInt(), add nextLine() to consume the leftover Enter
print() stays on same line, println() moves to next line

concepts.name: Arithmetic Operators
concepts.explanation: Basic mathematical operations.
concepts.code:
int a = 10, b = 3;
int sum = a + b;        // 13
int diff = a - b;       // 7
int product = a * b;    // 30
int quotient = a / b;   // 3 (integer division)
int remainder = a % b;  // 1 (modulo)

a++; // Increment: a = 11
b--; // Decrement: b = 2
keyPoints: / performs integer division if both operands are integers

concepts.name: Comparison Operators
concepts.explanation: Compare values and return boolean.
concepts.code:
int x = 5, y = 10;
boolean isEqual = (x == y);      // false
boolean notEqual = (x != y);     // true
boolean greater = (x > y);       // false
boolean less = (x < y);          // true
boolean greaterEq = (x >= 5);    // true
boolean lessEq = (y <= 10);      // true

concepts.name: Logical Operators
concepts.explanation: Combine boolean expressions.
keyPoints: && (AND): true only if both are true
|| (OR): true if at least one is true

concepts.name: if / else if / else
concepts.explanation: Execute code based on conditions.
keyPoints: First true condition executes, rest are skipped

concepts.name: Ternary Operator
concepts.explanation: Compact if-else in a single line.

concepts.name: switch Statement
concepts.explanation: Multi-way branch based on value.
keyPoints: Works with int, char, String, enum

concepts.name: for Loop
concepts.explanation: Iterate a fixed number of times.

concepts.name: while Loop
concepts.explanation: Loop while condition is true.
keyPoints: May not execute at all if condition false

concepts.name: do-while Loop
concepts.explanation: Execute at least once, then check condition.

concepts.name: for-each Loop
concepts.explanation: Iterate through arrays or collections.

concepts.name: break and continue
concepts.explanation: Control loop execution flow.

concepts.name: Array Declaration & Initialization
concepts.explanation: Fixed-size collection of elements of same type.
keyPoints: Array size is fixed after creation

concepts.name: Multi-dimensional Arrays
concepts.explanation: Arrays of arrays for matrix-like structures.
keyPoints: Can have jagged arrays (rows of different lengths)

concepts.name: Classes and Objects
concepts.explanation: A class is like a cookie cutter — it defines the shape. An object is the actual cookie you cut from it. You can make many cookies (objects) from one cutter (class), each with their own color/flavor (data).
keyPoints: Class = blueprint (defines what info and abilities objects have)
Object = actual instance (a real 'thing' with its own data)
this = 'the current object I'm working with' (helps avoid confusion)
Constructor = special method called when you say 'new'

concepts.name: Encapsulation
concepts.explanation: Think of a bank vault. You can't just reach in and grab money — you go through a teller (methods) who checks if you're allowed. Encapsulation hides your data (private) and controls access through getters/setters.
keyPoints: private = only this class can access (the vault door)
public = anyone can access (the teller window)
Getters let outside code READ your data safely
Setters let outside code WRITE your data with validation

concepts.name: Inheritance
concepts.explanation: Inheritance is like genetics — a child inherits traits from their parent. In Java, a child class automatically gets all the public/protected stuff from its parent, then can add its own features. You get code reuse for free!
keyPoints: extends = 'this class inherits from that class'
Child gets all public/protected members automatically
Java is single-inheritance only (one parent) — use interfaces for 'multiple'
super.method() calls the parent's version of a method

concepts.name: Polymorphism - Method Overriding
concepts.explanation: Child class provides specific implementation of parent method.
keyPoints: Called method determined at runtime (dynamic binding)

concepts.name: Polymorphism - Method Overloading
concepts.explanation: Multiple methods with same name but different parameters.
keyPoints: Return type alone doesn't distinguish methods

concepts.name: Abstract Classes
concepts.explanation: Cannot be instantiated; used as base for other classes.
keyPoints: Cannot create objects of abstract class
Can have abstract and concrete methods
Child must implement all abstract methods
Use when classes share common behavior

concepts.name: Interfaces
concepts.explanation: Contract specifying what methods a class must implement.
keyPoints: All methods public and abstract by default
Java 8+: interfaces can have default methods

concepts.name: ArrayList
concepts.explanation: Dynamic array that can grow/shrink.

concepts.name: HashMap
concepts.explanation: Key-value pairs for fast lookups.

concepts.name: HashSet
concepts.explanation: Collection of unique elements.

concepts.name: LinkedList
concepts.explanation: Doubly-linked list for efficient insertions/deletions.
keyPoints: Can be used as Stack or Queue

concepts.name: try-catch-finally
concepts.explanation: Handle errors gracefully without crashing.
keyPoints: try: Code that may throw exception
Multiple catch blocks for different exceptions

concepts.name: throw and throws
concepts.explanation: Manually throw exceptions or declare method throws.
keyPoints: Checked exceptions must be handled or declared

concepts.name: Custom Exceptions
concepts.explanation: Create your own exception types.

concepts.name: String Methods
concepts.explanation: Common operations on String objects.

concepts.name: StringBuilder
concepts.explanation: Mutable string for efficient modifications.
keyPoints: More efficient for concatenation in loops
Methods modify the object in place

concepts.name: Stream Operations
concepts.explanation: Functional-style operations on collections.
keyPoints: reduce: Combine elements to single value

concepts.name: Lambda Syntax
concepts.explanation: Concise way to write anonymous functions.

concepts.name: Generic Classes
concepts.explanation: Create type-safe classes that work with any type.
keyPoints: T is type parameter (can be any name)
Provides type safety at compile time
Common conventions: T (Type), E (Element), K (Key), V (Value)

concepts.name: Generic Methods
concepts.explanation: Methods that work with different types.

concepts.name: Bounded Type Parameters
concepts.explanation: Restrict types that can be used as type arguments.
keyPoints: extends limits type to specific class/interface
Can access methods of bound type
Multiple bounds: T extends Class & Interface1 & Interface2
Enables more specific operations on generic types

concepts.name: Wildcards
concepts.explanation: Flexible type parameters for generic methods.
keyPoints: Use when you don't need to know exact type

concepts.name: TreeMap
concepts.explanation: Sorted map implementation using Red-Black tree.
keyPoints: Keys sorted in natural order or by Comparator
O(log n) for add, remove, contains

concepts.name: TreeSet
concepts.explanation: Sorted set implementation, no duplicates.

concepts.name: Queue & Deque
concepts.explanation: FIFO queue and double-ended queue.
keyPoints: Can be used as Stack or Queue

concepts.name: PriorityQueue
concepts.explanation: Queue where elements are ordered by priority.
keyPoints: Elements ordered by natural order or Comparator
O(log n) for offer and poll

concepts.name: Reading Files
concepts.explanation: Read text files using various methods.
keyPoints: Files.readAllLines: Simple, reads all at once

concepts.name: Writing Files
concepts.explanation: Write text to files.
keyPoints: BufferedWriter: Better performance for large files
Use append mode (true) to add to existing file

concepts.name: File Operations
concepts.explanation: Common file and directory operations.
keyPoints: mkdir() creates one dir, mkdirs() creates nested

concepts.name: Serialization
concepts.explanation: Save and restore objects to/from files.

concepts.name: Creating Threads
concepts.explanation: Two ways to create threads in Java.
keyPoints: Runnable interface preferred over extending Thread
start() creates new thread, run() executes in current thread
Use lambda for concise thread creation

concepts.name: Thread Synchronization
concepts.explanation: Prevent race conditions with synchronized.
keyPoints: join() waits for thread to complete

concepts.name: ExecutorService
concepts.explanation: Manage thread pools efficiently.

concepts.name: CompletableFuture
concepts.explanation: Asynchronous programming with chaining.

concepts.name: LocalDate, LocalTime, LocalDateTime
concepts.explanation: Work with dates and times without time zones.

concepts.name: ZonedDateTime
concepts.explanation: Date and time with time zone information.

concepts.name: Period & Duration
concepts.explanation: Measure date and time differences.

concepts.name: Wrapper Classes & Autoboxing
concepts.explanation: Object wrappers for primitive types.
keyPoints: Each primitive has wrapper: Integer, Double, Boolean, etc.
Use equals() for wrapper comparison, not ==

concepts.name: Optional for Null Safety
concepts.explanation: Avoid NullPointerException with Optional.
keyPoints: Optional.of(): Value must not be null

concepts.name: Math Class
concepts.explanation: Mathematical operations and constants.

concepts.name: Random Class
concepts.explanation: Generate random numbers with more control.
keyPoints: Random class offers more control than Math.random()
Can set seed for reproducible sequences

concepts.name: var Keyword (Java 10+)
concepts.explanation: Let Java figure out the type for you. It's not dynamic typing — the type is still fixed at compile time, just inferred. Great for long generic types, but don't overuse it — readability matters!
keyPoints: Type is determined at compile time — still strongly typed!
Only for local variables with initializers (not fields or params)
Makes code cleaner when type is obvious from the right side
Avoid when type isn't clear: var x = getValue(); // What's x?

concepts.name: Records (Java 16+)
concepts.explanation: Tired of writing boilerplate for data classes? Records are Java's way of saying 'just hold this data.' You get constructor, getters, equals(), hashCode(), and toString() for free. Perfect for DTOs!
keyPoints: Records are immutable — fields are final, no setters
Auto-generates: constructor, getters, equals(), hashCode(), toString()
Getters use field name (not getField()) — e.g., person.name()
Perfect for DTOs, API responses, and value objects

concepts.name: Text Blocks (Java 15+)
concepts.explanation: Multi-line strings without escape character nightmares. Finally, you can write JSON, SQL, or HTML that actually looks readable! The opening """ must be on its own line.
keyPoints: Opening """ must be followed by newline
Indentation relative to closing """ (trailing whitespace trimmed)
No escape needed for quotes inside

concepts.name: Pattern Matching for instanceof (Java 16+)
concepts.explanation: No more casting after instanceof! Java now creates the variable for you in one step. It's the little things that make code cleaner.
keyPoints: Variable is automatically cast and available in the scope
Works with && conditions but NOT with ||
Foundation for more pattern matching features in future Java

concepts.name: Sealed Classes (Java 17+)
concepts.explanation: Control exactly which classes can extend yours. It's like an invite-only party for inheritance. Useful when you know all the subtypes and want the compiler to help you catch missing cases.
keyPoints: sealed = 'only these specific classes can extend me'
Permitted subclasses must be final, sealed, or non-sealed
Enables exhaustive pattern matching in switch
Great for representing fixed sets of types (like AST nodes)
